content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 22 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 111v–111r (LC PDF pp. 219–220) | **Source Images:** DEU_022_page_001.jpg, DEU_022_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 156–157 (pa_0156–pa_0157)

---

**1** You shall not see your brother's ox or his sheep going astray and ignore them. You shall bring them back to your brother. **2** And if your brother is not near you, or if you do not know him, you shall bring the animal into your house, and it shall remain with you until your brother seeks it. Then you shall return it to him. **3** And you shall do the same with his donkey; you shall do the same with his garment; and you shall do the same with any lost thing of your brother's, which he loses and you find. You may not ignore it.

**4** You shall not see your brother's donkey or his ox fallen down by the road and ignore them. You shall surely help him to lift them up.

**5** A woman shall not wear a man's garment, nor shall a man put on a woman's garment, for whoever does these things is an abomination to the LORD your God.

**6** If you happen upon a bird's nest along the road, in any tree or on the ground, with young ones or eggs, and the mother sitting on the young or on the eggs, you shall not take the mother with the young. **7** You shall surely let the mother go, but the young you may take for yourself, that it may go well with you and that you may live long.

**8** When you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, so that you do not bring bloodguilt on your house if anyone falls from it.

**9** You shall not sow your vineyard with two kinds of seed, lest the whole yield be forfeited—the crop that you have sown and the yield of the vineyard. **10** You shall not plow with an ox and a donkey together. **11** You shall not wear cloth of wool and linen mixed together.

**12** You shall make tassels on the four corners of your garment with which you cover yourself.

**13** If any man takes a wife and goes in to her and then hates her **14** and brings charges against her and gives her a bad name, saying, "I took this woman, and when I came near her, I did not find in her the signs of virginity," **15** then the father of the young woman and her mother shall take and bring out the evidence of the young woman's virginity to the elders of the city at the gate. **16** And the father of the young woman shall say to the elders, "I gave my daughter to this man to be his wife, and he hates her; **17** and behold, he has charged her with shameful conduct, saying, 'I did not find in your daughter the signs of virginity.' And yet these are the signs of my daughter's virginity." And they shall spread the garment before the elders of the city. **18** Then the elders of that city shall take the man and discipline him. **19** And they shall fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the father of the young woman, because he has given a bad name to a virgin of Israel. And she shall be his wife; he may not divorce her all his days.

**20** But if this charge is true, and the signs of virginity were not found in the young woman, **21** then they shall bring out the young woman to the door of her father's house, and the men of her city shall stone her to death with stones, because she has done a disgraceful thing in Israel by practicing prostitution in her father's house. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**22** If a man is found lying with a woman who is the wife of another man, both of them shall die—the man who lay with the woman and the woman. So you shall purge the evil from Israel.

**23** If there is a young woman who is a virgin betrothed to a man, and another man finds her in the city and lies with her, **24** then you shall bring them both out to the gate of that city, and you shall stone them to death with stones—the young woman because she did not cry out in the city, and the man because he violated his neighbor's wife. So you shall purge the evil from your midst.

**25** But if the man meets the betrothed young woman in the open country, and the man seizes her and lies with her, then only the man who lay with her shall die. **26** But you shall do nothing to the young woman; she has committed no sin worthy of death. For this case is like that of a man who attacks his neighbor and murders him, **27** because he found her in the open country. Though the betrothed young woman cried out, there was no one to save her.

**28** If a man finds a young woman who is a virgin who is not betrothed, and seizes her and lies with her, and they are found, **29** then the man who lay with her shall give to the father of the young woman fifty shekels of silver, and she shall be his wife, because he has violated her. He may not divorce her all his days.

**30** A man shall not take his father's wife, so that he does not uncover his father's skirt.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**LOGOS — Formal Equivalence Analysis, Deuteronomy 22**

**v.1-3 "ignore them" (hithalalemta):** The Hithpael of alam = to hide oneself, to feign blindness. "Ignore" captures the willful act of pretending not to see. LXX: παρόψῃ ("pass by/overlook"), similar concept. LOGOS: "ignore" is slightly stronger, preserving the deliberate nature of the inaction.

**v.5 "a man's garment" (kli gever):** Lit. "the article/implement of a man/warrior." The word kli is broader than garment—it can mean vessel, weapon, or equipment. "Garment" is the traditional rendering; some read "the equipment/arms of a man." The pairing with simlat ishah (woman's garment/robe) in the parallel clause suggests clothing is primary, though the broader sense may be intended. LOGOS renders "garment" following the parallel structure. LXX: σκεύη ἀνδρός ("the utensils/items of a man"), similarly broader.

**v.5 "abomination" (to'evah):** The strong ritual-moral term used for practices that violate the created order. Retained without softening.

**v.6-7 "Let the mother go" (shalleach teshalach):** The cognate infinitive construction intensifies the command: "you shall surely let go." LXX: ἀποστολῇ ἀποστελεῖς ("you shall surely send away"). The provision for the mother bird is grounded in the same creational logic as land-rest (Lev 25) and first-fruits: human use must not destroy the source of future blessing.

**v.8 "parapet" (ma'akeh):** A low wall or railing around a flat roof. LXX: στεφάνην ("crown/border-wall"), corresponding. The flat-roofed houses of the ancient Near East meant the roof was a habitable surface.

**v.9 "forfeited" (tiqdash):** The Qal of qadash in this context means "to become holy = set apart for the sanctuary = effectively forfeit to the owner." The entire yield becomes qodesh (dedicated/set apart) and is removed from ordinary use. LXX: ἁγιασθῇ ("be sanctified"), preserving the same legal concept. LOGOS: "forfeited" communicates the practical outcome while "dedicated/set apart" would be more literal.

**v.11 "cloth of wool and linen mixed together" (sha'atnez):** The technical term sha'atnez is preserved in the Masoretic text; its precise etymology is uncertain (possibly Egyptian loanword). LXX: κίβδηλον ("bastard/spurious/mixed"), a different term entirely. The prohibition on mixture (kilayim) extends to seeds (v.9), plowing teams (v.10), and fabrics.

**v.12 "tassels" (gedilim):** The term here for tassels; Numbers 15 uses tsitsit. Both refer to the fringes worn on garment corners. LXX: στρεπτά ("twisted cords"), different from the LXX of Numbers, suggesting awareness of a distinction.

**v.14 "signs of virginity" (betulim):** The plural noun betulim refers to evidence of virginity, traditionally understood as the bloodstained garment from the wedding night. This is a contentious term medically and legally; LOGOS follows the MT's concrete language.

**v.19 "a hundred shekels of silver" (me'ah kesef):** Double the bride price of 50 shekels (v.29), signifying the punitive character of the fine for defaming a virgin of Israel.

**v.21 "practicing prostitution" (liznot):** The Qal infinitive of zanah. In her father's house = prior to marriage, she engaged in sexual relations. "Practicing prostitution" renders the verb's primary sense, though the action described may be pre-marital intercourse rather than formal prostitution. LXX: πορνεῦσαι ("to act as a prostitute"), same verb root.

**v.28 "seizes her" (taphas):** A forceful verb meaning to grab, lay hold of. This verse describes rape of an unbetrothed virgin. "Seizes" preserves the coercive nature of the act, distinguishing it from consensual intercourse. The law imposes marriage as a consequence (protecting the woman's future social standing) and prohibits future divorce. LXX: βιάσηται ("he forces/rapes her"), even more explicit about the coercion.

**v.30 "uncover his father's skirt" (lo yigalleh kenaf aviv):** The idiom for sexual access to a woman under a man's protective authority. "Skirt" (kanaf = wing/edge of a garment) functions as a metonym for sexual access and protection (cf. Ruth 3:9; Ezek 16:8). LXX: οὐ λήμψεται ἄνθρωπος τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ ("a man shall not take his father's wife"), making the referent explicit. MT's idiom is retained as more expressive.

**Note on Chapter 22's Numbering:** The MT verse count for this chapter totals 30 verses; the final verse (v.30 in MT) is 23:1 in some English versifications. LOGOS follows the MT verse numbering as the primary source.

— LOGOS
